items.title: When did we start using passports? - Kristin Surak
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Trace the history of passports to find out why we need these documents and what challenges travelers face within this system.

--

Passports are not the first form of travel documents: in Ancient Egypt, travelers carried “letters of safe conduct” issued by rulers; in Han China and Tudor England, foreigners were issued travel passes. However, for much of human history, borders have been relatively fluid, and travel lacked bureaucratic requirements. So, what changed? Kristin Surak details the history of the passport system.

Lesson by Kristin Surak, directed by Tomás Pichardo-Espaillat.

items.title: We’ve Been Wrong About the Trojan Horse
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>The legendary tale of the Trojan War has captivated people for thousands of years, filled with Greek gods, epic battles, mysterious prophecies, and the famous giant wooden horse said to have brought down the city of Troy. But was the Trojan Horse real, or is it one of history's greatest myths?

On this episode of Weird History, we examine the possibility that historians have misunderstood the Trojan Horse all along. Was it truly a massive wooden horse hiding Greek soldiers, or could it have been something entirely different?

Do you think there was really a Trojan Horse? Let us know in the comments.

(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.) 


Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews



#trojanhorse #greece #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: Why Scientists Think This Is Our Strongest Evidence of Aliens
items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>Did we find evidence of aliens in old astronomical records?

We might not be alone in the Universe. While combing through data from 1949, scientists spotted something impossible: strange, vanishing lights. They look exactly like satellites, but were documented decades before humanity ever launched anything into space. What could they be? Could this be evidence of advanced alien technology?

items.title: Secrets of the Winchester Mystery House
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>The Winchester Mystery House is one of America's most famous haunted mansions. Built by Sarah Winchester, heiress to the Winchester Rifle fortune, this bizarre mansion is filled with secret passageways, staircases that lead nowhere, and rooms designed to confuse both visitors and, according to legend, restless spirits.

In this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift, we explore the chilling legends and fascinating history behind the mysterious Winchester House. Was it built to escape a deadly curse? Is it truly haunted, or is there a more rational explanation behind its strange architecture? 

What's your take on the ghosts of Winchester Mystery House? Let us know in the comments.

Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#hauntedmansion #paranormal #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: How does the space station never run out of oxygen? - Alvaro Romero-Calvo and Theo St Francis
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Explore the incredible systems that create breathable air in space, and how we can adapt that process for future expeditions.

--

For over 25 years, the International Space Station’s air supply systems have revolutionized space missions. Yet the need for regular maintenance, repairs, and upgrades has pushed engineers to develop even more reliable systems for future spacecraft. How do we create breathable air in space? And what does that look like for longer expeditions? Alvaro Romero-Calvo and Theo St Francis investigate.

Lesson by Alvaro Romero-Calvo and Theo St Francis, directed by Lazy Chief.

items.title: Why This Extremely Expensive Doll Is So Controversial
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>When you think of an American Girl doll, you probably think of wholesome history lessons, inspiring role models, and childhood nostalgia.

But despite their educational mission, American Girl has found itself at the center of controversy again and again. From surprising historical inaccuracies to culture wars and collector backlash, the beloved doll brand has repeatedly sparked debates no toy company ever expected.

On this episode of Weird History we dive into the doll’s history and try and figure out why the  beloved line can’t stay out of trouble.

(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.) 

Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#noai #americangirl #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: Hubble Spotted Something Huge Hurtling Through Space
items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>Astronomers spotted a runaway supermassive black hole. 
Try Brilliant’s new interactive tutor for free and get 20% off an annual Premium subscription at https://brilliant.org/astrum/

▀▀▀▀▀▀

For the first time ever, scientists have spotted a runaway supermassive black hole hurtling through space… alone. Leaving a trail of newborn stars in its wake, this cosmic giant is defying everything we know about how black holes behave. In this video, we’re diving into the mysteries scientists are racing to solve: How is this black hole creating new stars? Why is it not inside a galaxy? And how did it escape?

items.title: The Real Life Dexter Copycat Killer
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>The disturbing phenomenon of copycat killers has blurred the line between entertainment and reality for decades. In one of the most chilling true crime cases, Mark Andrew Twitchell, an aspiring serial killer from Canada, became obsessed with the hit TV series Dexter, modeling his crimes after television's infamous vigilante.

On this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift, we take a look at the bizarre and horrifying story of how a TV antihero influenced a real-life killer, the shocking evidence investigators uncovered, and why the case became one of the most infamous examples of life imitating art.

Are you a fan of Dexter? Let us know in the comments.


Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#dexter #truecrimestories #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: This isn’t the original Statue of Liberty
items.author: Real Engineering

items.title: Psychedelics, mind control, radiation-eating: The secret superpowers of fungi - Max G. Levy
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Dig into the vast and wild world of fungi, and explore how these organisms evolved to play a range of essential ecological roles.

--

The diverse fungal kingdom, which includes everything from baker’s yeast to edible mushrooms to foot infections, has long defied assumptions. Scientists estimate that somewhere between 2 and 12 million species of fungi remain undiscovered— either because they’re microscopic, live hidden within other species, or for other unknown reasons. Max G. Levy digs into the vast and wild world of fungi.

Lesson by Max G. Levy, directed by Petya Zlateva, Compote Collective.

items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>Will Pluto ever be a planet again?
Check out Ouranos with this link to find the best stargazing conditions and make the most of your next observing session: http://ouranos-app.com/download - start with a 7 day-free trial or grab 30% off an annual subscription

▀▀▀▀▀▀

This Astrum supercut explores our favourite dwarf planet: Pluto. We’ll discover why its planetary status really got revoked, take a closer look at what’s hiding under the surface, and reveal the surprising discoveries on its largest moon, Charon. 

To those returning and new to the channel: This video is a supercut of some of Astrum’s best Pluto videos, plus new and updated content. We’ve edited this into a new seamless video, remastered in 4K resolution.

items.title: Why The German Army Feared The Night Witches
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>On the dark battlefields of World War II, German soldiers feared an enemy they could barely see and rarely heard... until it was too late. Out of the blackness came a faint whooshing sound. Seconds later, bombs rained from the sky, buildings crumbled, and chaos erupted across the Eastern Front. On this episode of Weird History we take a look at the mysterious Night Witches: an elite squadron of all-female Soviet pilots who became one of the most feared forces of World War II. Flying outdated wooden biplanes on daring nighttime bombing missions, they terrorized German troops, earned countless military honors, and cemented their place among history's most legendary warriors. Have you ever heard of the Night Witches? Let us know in the comments. 


(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.) 



Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#wwii #nightwitches #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: The Strangest Medical Stories We Could Find | Compilation
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>The History of Medicine is a wild one. How humans figured out cures and even developed surgical procedures that saved lives! Often times what seemed to work in the past was later found to be detrimental to health and then abandoned. Today on Weird History we go into various aspects of the history of Medicine, the good, the bad, and the out-right miraculous!
What is your favorite part about the history of all things medical? Let us know in the comments!


Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

items.title: How a broken heart affects your body - Roni Shanoada
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Dig into broken heart syndrome, and find out how grief and traumatic events can affect your body and health.

--


Losing a loved one can be one of the most traumatic experiences of a person’s life. Grief brings on a whole host of strong emotions that impact both our mental and physical health. But is it possible for these powerful feelings to endanger your life? In other words, can you actually die from a broken heart? Roni Shanoada explores how traumatic events can affect your body.

Lesson by Roni Shanoada, directed by Joana Sus, NOMINT.

items.title: This Moon Collision Did Something Crazy on Earth
items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>What created Tycho crater?
Get a 10% discount on your next VisiSolar purchase when you use code ASTRUM2026 or the following link: https://visisolar.com/discount/ASTRUM2026

▀▀▀▀▀▀

Our Moon is covered with scars, but one is more recognisable than any other: Tycho. Formed in a violent impact that sent debris flying toward Earth, this iconic crater holds clues to the Moon’s hidden secrets. In this video, we’re digging into the evidence to discover what collided with the Moon, what happened here on Earth, and why the timeline still puzzles scientists today.

▀▀▀▀▀▀

2:24 Central Mountain
4:45 Lava Tsunami 
7:43 Aftermath
8:39 Landslide 
10:59 Raining Glass 
12:03 Seen From Earth
13:08 Raining Glass on Earth
13:57 How Old Is Tycho?
15:55 Fresh Crater
17:17 Earth-Moon Exchange

▀▀▀▀▀▀

items.title: The Ghosts Of Alcatraz
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Alcatraz, also known as "The Rock," was a maximum-security fortress where only the most dangerous and clever criminals were sent. One of the most infamous prisons in American history, it sat alone on a rocky island in San Francisco Bay and was once considered escape-proof. But decades after the prison closed, questions still linger. Did some prisoners actually escape? What secrets remain hidden behind its crumbling walls? And could Alcatraz be a place where even death isn't enough to set you free?

In this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift we take a look at the strange history of Alcatraz, the prison that may never let go. 

Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#haunted #alcatraz #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: What causes avalanches, and can you survive them? - Simon Trautman
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Explore the three conditions needed to trigger an avalanche, and what makes these natural disasters so hard to survive.

--

Avalanches are the rapid descent of snow, ice, rock, or a combination of these down a slope. They can be destructive natural disasters and personal tragedies: killing an average of 27 people annually in the US. These alpine nightmares don’t just awaken out of nowhere; they need three specific conditions. So, what causes an avalanche? And is there any way to survive? Simon Trautman investigates.

Lesson by Simon Trautman, directed by Denys Spolitak.

items.title: Why This Princess's Life Was No Fairy Tale
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Princess Diana was one of the most beloved and iconic royals in modern history. But behind the fairy-tale image, lies a far darker story. Long before the paparazzi, royal scandals, and the tragic Paris car crash that shocked the world, Diana's life was marked by loneliness, heartbreak, and immense pressure.

In this episode of Weird History, we're uncovering the true story of Princess Diana, from her troubled childhood and turbulent royal marriage to the events that made her a global icon and ultimately led to one of the most tragic endings in modern history.

Were you a fan of Princess Di? Let us know in the comments.

(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.)


Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#princessdianaforever #britishmonarchy #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: The Weird History of Sports and Games | Compilation
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Sports, games, and entertainment of the masses have been part of the human experience since, well, humans existed! ...showcasing  physical prowess, raw talent, or even taking time of a busy schedule for a board game or two. Join us as we get into all things sports, spectating, and many other things! For example, what was the average spectator experience at the old Roman Colosseum like? How did Milton Bradley start an incredible board game empire? ...and what was that whole Atari E.T. video game debacle all about? These and many more subjects in today's compilation - let us you your favorite game or sport in the comments!

Chapters

00:00:00 - What It Was Like to Be an Ancient Olympian
00:11:38 - What Being a Spectator at the Rome Colosseum Was Like
00:22:23 - How A Baseball Player Became a World War II Assassin
00:34:02 - The Family-Friendly Milton Bradley Corporation Has A Bizarre History
00:44:14 - How the ET Video Game Ended Atari
00:54:33 - When The Olympics Became A Hostage Crisis
01:04:39 - Facts About Bobby Fischer

Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews</article>

items.title: Should you rinse your dishes before placing them in the dishwasher? - Rachel Yang
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Take a look inside a dishwasher to explore how these machines work, and find out how to get the cleanest dishes from every use.

--

After years of hosting lavish dinner parties and watching her dishware get chipped during handwashing, American socialite Josephine Cochrane was convinced the world needed a better way to clean dishes. So, she built a prototype for a dishwashing contraption. And the design would become the model for our modern dishwashers. So, how do they work? Rachel Yang takes a look inside the machine.

Lesson by Rachel Yang, directed by Aaron Augenblick, Augenblick Studios.

items.title: Sagittarius A* Might Not Be a Black Hole
items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>A new theory suggests Sagittarius A* might not be a black hole after all.
Start speaking a new language in 3 weeks with Babbel 🎉. Get up to 55% OFF ➡️ Here: https://bit.ly/AstrumJun26

▀▀▀▀▀▀

A new theory is challenging everything we know about Sagittarius A*, the supermassive object at the heart of our galaxy. For decades, physicists have been certain it’s a black hole. They have observed its effects and even built a planet-sized telescope just to image it. But now, a mind-bending theory is turning our understanding upside down. What if Sagittarius A* isn’t a black hole at all, but something far stranger?

▀▀▀▀▀▀

items.title: The Real Nightmare That Inspired Open Water
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Imagine going on a 40-minute scuba dive in the middle of the ocean and then surfacing to discover your boat has vanished. You’ve been accidentally left behind, surrounded by endless open water with nowhere to go and no way to call for help. Panic sets in, and every minute becomes a fight for survival.

On this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift we take a look at the terrifying true story of Tom and Eileen Lonergan, divers who were left stranded at sea and learned the hard way how a simple mistake can turn an ordinary scuba trip into a real-life nightmare.

Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#deepsea #horror #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: How to attack (and defend) a medieval castle - Connor C. Wilson
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Dig into the history of medieval European castles, and explore how the design of these formidable fortresses evolved over time.

--

Medieval European castles weren’t just for passive defense and withstanding enemy onslaughts; they were symbols of power, private residences, and hubs of governance. Castles regularly served as bases for launching attacks and as a way to control local populations. So, how were these formidable fortresses built? Connor C. Wilson explores how the design of these structures evolved over time.

Lesson by Connor C. Wilson, directed by Layron DeJarnette.
